topic: An employee discovers the company is being sold
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the grain of his desk with a fingertip, the cheap veneer feeling slick and oily under his touch. The meeting invite, innocuous enough—a review of Q3 projections—had been a Trojan horse. Hidden within the attached documents, a slide deck detailed a complete divestiture plan. His stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming just beneath his ribs. He reread the figures, the projected layoffs, the strategic shift towards 'streamlining operations.' It wasn't just restructuring; it was a handover. A clean sweep. He pushed his chair back, the scrape of metal on linoleum sounding unnervingly loud in the otherwise silent office. He looked around at the cubicles, at the faces of his colleagues—faces that seemed suddenly vacant, like mannequins awaiting replacement. A slow, deliberate breath escaped him, tasting like ash. He gathered his personal files, his movements stiff, mechanical. He didn't bother to look at anyone. The air in the room felt thick, suffocating.

[story 2]

“So, we’re moving forward with the acquisition?” Sarah asked, her voice bright, entirely devoid of the cautious tone Elias had expected. She leaned over his shoulder, her perfume cloying and artificial. Elias felt a wave of nausea rise, a physical tightening in his throat that made swallowing difficult. He kept his gaze fixed on the monitor, refusing to meet her eyes. The presentation slides flashed across the screen: glossy renderings of new logos, promises of 'unprecedented growth.' It all sounded hollow, a carefully constructed façade built on sand. When the CEO spoke about synergy and market penetration, Elias felt a profound sense of betrayal, a deep, internal churning. He gripped the edge of his keyboard until his knuckles turned white, the pressure a desperate anchor against the rising tide of resentment. He finally spoke, his voice flat, “What does this mean for the people who actually build this place?” The silence that followed was heavy, weighted with unspoken complicity.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights hummed with an aggressive, high-pitched whine, mirroring the frantic buzzing in Elias’s own mind. He found the folder tucked deep in a shared drive, labeled simply: 'Exit Strategy.' Opening it felt like defusing a bomb. The documents outlined the sale, the valuation, the timeline. Every paragraph was a calculated betrayal of the years he had poured into this organization. A bitter, metallic taste coated his tongue. He stood up abruptly, knocking his chair slightly askew. The sound echoed in the open-plan area. He didn't need to read further; the implications were stark and brutal. He walked towards the window, looking out at the cityscape, the towering glass structures seeming cold and indifferent. He pressed his palms against the cool glass, trying to physically anchor himself against the sheer scale of the impending collapse. A tremor ran through his hands, and he squeezed his eyes shut, fighting the urge to simply walk out and never look back.
